items.title: Azure resilience and continuity guidance
items.summary: Use Azure guidance to review resilience, recovery, and continuity choices.
items.whyItMatters: Azure-hosted systems need platform-aware reliability patterns, not generic cloud advice.
items.whenToApply: Apply when the architecture uses Azure services or Azure-hosted production workloads.
items.guidance: Check failure recovery, backup planning, workload isolation, and continuity readiness for Azure-dependent services.

items.title: Azure security, cost, and operations guidance
items.summary: Use Azure guidance to balance security controls, cost choices, and operational readiness.
items.whyItMatters: Cloud-native designs often fail when identity, secrets, or observability are left vague.
items.whenToApply: Apply when Azure services, managed identity, Blob Storage, or Key Vault are present.
items.guidance: Review identity boundaries, secrets handling, telemetry, automation, and right-sized managed services.

items.title: AWS reliability and elasticity guidance
items.summary: Use AWS guidance to review scaling, tolerance to failure, and recovery design.
items.whyItMatters: AWS-heavy systems benefit from service-aware resilience and elasticity decisions.
items.whenToApply: Apply when the architecture uses AWS services or compares against AWS deployment patterns.
items.guidance: Review scaling, failure isolation, backup and restore, and recovery time expectations.

items.title: AWS operations, security, and cost guidance
items.summary: Use AWS guidance to check operational discipline, platform security, and spend efficiency.
items.whyItMatters: Without strong operations and guardrails, AWS systems can grow cost and risk quickly.
items.whenToApply: Apply when AWS platform services, partner APIs, or production workloads are in scope.
items.guidance: Review operations, identity, infrastructure protection, telemetry, and cost awareness.

items.title: ISO 25010 system quality guidance
items.summary: Use ISO 25010 to review overall software quality and non-functional fit.
items.whyItMatters: Architecture quality problems often come from broad system-fit issues, not only infrastructure gaps.
items.whenToApply: Apply when maintainability, reliability, portability, or general product quality is important.
items.guidance: Review maintainability, reliability, performance efficiency, compatibility, and portability concerns.

items.title: ISO 25010 reliability and product fitness guidance
items.summary: Use ISO 25010 when the review needs a cloud-neutral view of whether the system is fit for purpose.
items.whyItMatters: It helps teams reason about product quality without tying every decision to one cloud provider.
items.whenToApply: Apply in cloud-neutral reviews or when architecture quality spans product behavior and operations.
items.guidance: Check whether the system supports intended use, tolerates faults, and remains understandable to evolve.

items.title: OWASP ASVS API and access-control guidance
items.summary: Use OWASP ASVS when the system exposes APIs or user-facing access paths.
items.whyItMatters: Public-facing application behavior creates security risk long before infrastructure fails.
items.whenToApply: Apply when there are APIs, external users, partner integrations, or admin access flows.
items.guidance: Review authentication, authorization, input validation, session safety, and API exposure controls.

items.title: OWASP ASVS data protection and logging guidance
items.summary: Use OWASP ASVS to review data protection, logging safety, and cryptographic hygiene.
items.whyItMatters: Sensitive systems need secure handling of data, secrets, and operational logs.
items.whenToApply: Apply when uploads, personal data, or privileged workflows are part of the architecture.
items.guidance: Check encryption, secret use, secure logging, safe error handling, and exposure of sensitive data.

items.title: TOGAF architecture governance guidance
items.summary: Use TOGAF when the architecture needs stronger governance and decision flow across a larger change program.
items.whyItMatters: Large organizations need a way to connect architecture intent, control, and execution over time.
items.whenToApply: Apply when the system is part of a roadmap-heavy or governance-heavy transformation.
items.guidance: Review governance forums, decision ownership, architecture checkpoints, and long-range capability planning.

items.title: TOGAF ADM phase guidance
items.summary: Use TOGAF ADM thinking to move from architecture vision to implementation and change control.
items.whyItMatters: Teams often have target-state ideas but weak transition planning and follow-through.
items.whenToApply: Apply when the architecture needs phased change planning, migration thinking, or operating-model alignment.
items.guidance: Review transition phases, implementation planning, and how architecture decisions survive delivery pressure.

items.title: SAFe value stream and delivery flow guidance
items.summary: Use SAFe guidance when architecture affects flow across many delivery teams.
items.whyItMatters: Cross-team architecture can slow delivery if release flow and coordination are not designed intentionally.
items.whenToApply: Apply when multiple teams depend on shared architecture decisions or platform capabilities.
items.guidance: Review how architecture supports value flow, coordination, and cross-team integration.

items.title: SAFe system architecture coordination guidance
items.summary: Use SAFe system architecture guidance to align technical direction across product and platform teams.
items.whyItMatters: Shared architecture without coordination creates bottlenecks, duplicated work, and unstable releases.
items.whenToApply: Apply when one platform serves many product teams or release coordination is a real concern.
items.guidance: Review platform coordination, integration boundaries, release dependencies, and system-team readiness.

items.title: ISO 27001 control and risk treatment guidance
items.summary: Use ISO 27001 guidance when the architecture needs stronger control thinking and risk treatment discipline.
items.whyItMatters: Security maturity improves when teams move from isolated controls to a repeatable management approach.
items.whenToApply: Apply when sensitive systems need access control, asset protection, or audit-friendly security decisions.
items.guidance: Review control coverage, risk treatment, access boundaries, asset handling, and security evidence needs.

items.title: ISO 27001 incident and continuity guidance
items.summary: Use ISO 27001 guidance to review security incident readiness and continuity expectations.
items.whyItMatters: Controls are incomplete if the team cannot respond and recover in a disciplined way.
items.whenToApply: Apply when outage handling, incident response, or audit evidence matters.
items.guidance: Review operational security routines, continuity planning, and accountability for security incidents.

items.title: GDPR data minimization and purpose guidance
items.summary: Use GDPR guidance when the system stores personal data or serves European users.
items.whyItMatters: Privacy obligations affect architecture choices like retention, data copies, and access paths.
items.whenToApply: Apply when personal data, document uploads, or customer data flows are in scope.
items.guidance: Review lawful processing, purpose limitation, data minimization, and where personal data is stored or copied.

items.title: GDPR retention and deletion guidance
items.summary: Use GDPR guidance to review how data is retained, deleted, and handled across borders.
items.whyItMatters: Privacy architecture is weak when deletion and lifecycle paths are undefined.
items.whenToApply: Apply when the system stores personal records, uploaded documents, or long-lived customer data.
items.guidance: Review data retention, deletion workflows, cross-border handling, and subject-right support.

items.title: SOC 2 trust and control evidence guidance
items.summary: Use SOC 2 guidance when customers expect proof of operational and security discipline.
items.whyItMatters: Trust-driven systems need evidence that controls exist and are actually followed.
items.whenToApply: Apply when enterprise customers care about security, uptime, and audit readiness.
items.guidance: Review control evidence, operational accountability, confidentiality handling, and service availability.

items.title: SOC 2 processing integrity and privacy guidance
items.summary: Use SOC 2 guidance to review whether the service behaves consistently and can prove it.
items.whyItMatters: Customers trust systems that can show reliable operations and controlled handling of user data.
items.whenToApply: Apply when the platform supports business workflows, customer trust reviews, or external service obligations.
items.guidance: Review change control, processing integrity, privacy handling, and evidence for repeatable operations.

items.title: Design for workload separation
items.summary: Split interactive, batch, and background workloads so one path does not block the rest.
items.whyItMatters: Shared workload paths become bottlenecks under growth or traffic spikes.
items.whenToApply: Apply when uploads, AI jobs, integrations, or background processing can grow faster than request traffic.
items.guidance: Separate synchronous user flows from heavier asynchronous work and isolate high-variance processing.

items.title: Prefer asynchronous processing for heavy tasks
items.summary: Move long-running work off the request path.
items.whyItMatters: Heavy operations can hurt user experience and throughput when they stay inline.
items.whenToApply: Apply for document extraction, media processing, large imports, and integration-heavy flows.
items.guidance: Use messaging, jobs, callbacks, or status tracking for work that is slow or bursty.

items.title: Remove single bottlenecks early
items.summary: Find the one component that will cap growth and address it before it becomes a crisis.
items.whyItMatters: One narrow dependency can erase the benefit of every other scaling decision.
items.whenToApply: Apply when many users, tenants, or pipelines share the same component.
items.guidance: Review shared data paths, single worker pools, and centralized services for hidden capacity limits.

items.title: Design for failure and recovery
items.summary: Expect failures and make recovery part of the design.
items.whyItMatters: Reliable systems recover quickly instead of pretending failures will not happen.
items.whenToApply: Apply to every production workload with meaningful customer or business impact.
items.guidance: Review fallback behavior, recovery paths, retry design, and operational failure handling.

items.title: Protect critical data with backup and restore paths
items.summary: Plan how important data is restored, not just how it is stored.
items.whyItMatters: Storage without tested restore paths is not enough for real resilience.
items.whenToApply: Apply when data loss would hurt customers, operations, or audit obligations.
items.guidance: Review backup scope, restore confidence, metadata recovery, and disaster recovery expectations.

items.title: Make dependencies observable and retry-safe
items.summary: Watch critical dependencies closely and handle temporary failure safely.
items.whyItMatters: Silent dependency failures spread quickly across the system.
items.whenToApply: Apply when the system depends on external APIs, queues, data stores, or background workers.
items.guidance: Review telemetry, timeout handling, retry logic, and dead-letter or compensating paths.

items.title: Protect identity and access boundaries
items.summary: Make it clear who can do what and where those boundaries are enforced.
items.whyItMatters: Weak access control creates high-impact incidents very quickly.
items.whenToApply: Apply when the system has users, admins, partners, or privileged actions.
items.guidance: Review authentication, authorization, role boundaries, and tenant separation.

items.title: Treat secrets as managed assets
items.summary: Store keys and credentials in managed secret systems.
items.whyItMatters: Hardcoded or loosely shared secrets create avoidable risk.
items.whenToApply: Apply whenever services call other services or handle privileged credentials.
items.guidance: Review secret storage, rotation, access scope, and operational handling of credentials.

items.title: Reduce attack surface at ingress and integration points
items.summary: Secure APIs, uploads, and outside entry paths first.
items.whyItMatters: Exposed boundaries are usually the first place attackers and failures appear.
items.whenToApply: Apply when the architecture accepts internet traffic, files, or partner integrations.
items.guidance: Review gateway patterns, validation, rate control, upload safety, and edge protection.

items.title: Keep boundaries clear and decisions documented
items.summary: Separate concerns and record important choices.
items.whyItMatters: Teams move faster when the architecture is easier to understand and explain.
items.whenToApply: Apply when many components or teams depend on the same architecture.
items.guidance: Review service boundaries, ownership, and whether decisions are captured as ADRs.

items.title: Prefer simple designs until complexity is justified
items.summary: Start lean and add complexity only when there is clear evidence for it.
items.whyItMatters: Unnecessary complexity slows delivery and raises operational burden.
items.whenToApply: Apply during early growth, MVP evolution, and platform redesign choices.
items.guidance: Review whether added services, orchestration, or indirection solve a real problem today.

items.title: Make systems easier to test and change
items.summary: Favor designs that support safe iteration.
items.whyItMatters: Architecture that cannot change cleanly becomes a long-term business drag.
items.whenToApply: Apply to shared services, pipelines, and integration-heavy systems.
items.guidance: Review coupling, interface clarity, test seams, and how safely components evolve.

items.title: Match service choice to workload value
items.summary: Spend more where it protects important outcomes, and less where it does not.
items.whyItMatters: Cheap choices can become expensive if they create outages, rework, or manual toil.
items.whenToApply: Apply when choosing storage, compute, messaging, or managed platform services.
items.guidance: Review whether service choices match business criticality, scale, and support burden.

items.title: Use lifecycle and retention controls
items.summary: Keep expensive data only as long as it still helps.
items.whyItMatters: Storage, logs, and artifacts quietly grow cost over time.
items.whenToApply: Apply to blobs, backups, audit data, media assets, and extracted documents.
items.guidance: Review archive, retention, deletion, and tiering policies for large or long-lived data.

items.title: Scale expensive capabilities deliberately
items.summary: Control the parts of the system that burn the most money.
items.whyItMatters: AI, media, and high-throughput processing can spike spend quickly.
items.whenToApply: Apply when compute-heavy analysis or storage-heavy workloads are present.
items.guidance: Review cost controls, quotas, concurrency limits, and where expensive processing is triggered.

items.title: Minimize sensitive data exposure
items.summary: Only keep and expose the sensitive data the system truly needs.
items.whyItMatters: Every extra copy of sensitive data increases privacy and audit risk.
items.whenToApply: Apply when personal, financial, regulated, or customer-sensitive data is present.
items.guidance: Review data copies, access scope, enrichment flows, and storage sprawl.

items.title: Keep evidence for audit and accountability
items.summary: Record the actions and controls that matter.
items.whyItMatters: Teams need to prove what happened, not just hope logs exist somewhere.
items.whenToApply: Apply when business review, customer trust, or regulation depends on traceability.
items.guidance: Review audit logging, evidence retention, review trails, and operational accountability.

items.title: Design retention and deletion intentionally
items.summary: Know how data is kept, archived, and removed.
items.whyItMatters: Privacy and governance obligations often depend on lifecycle control.
items.whenToApply: Apply when customer records, uploads, or employee data is stored.
items.guidance: Review retention periods, deletion flows, archived copies, and data subject handling.

items.title: Make health, logs, and traces visible
items.summary: Give operators enough signal to understand the system.
items.whyItMatters: Hidden failures take longer to diagnose and fix.
items.whenToApply: Apply to all critical services, queues, jobs, and user-facing APIs.
items.guidance: Review metrics, logs, tracing, alerts, and cross-service visibility.

items.title: Automate repeatable operational tasks
items.summary: Remove manual work where failure is predictable.
items.whyItMatters: Reliability drops when routine operations depend on memory or heroics.
items.whenToApply: Apply to deployment, maintenance, secret rotation, recovery, and support operations.
items.guidance: Review where manual steps still exist in routine or high-risk operations.

items.title: Prepare for incident response and change control
items.summary: Know how the team will respond when the system or a change goes wrong.
items.whyItMatters: Fast, calm response reduces customer and business impact.
items.whenToApply: Apply when the system supports production users or regulated workloads.
items.guidance: Review incident response, rollback paths, on-call readiness, and change visibility.

items.title: Consistency vs availability
items.summary: Choose between stronger consistency and broader availability during partial failure.
items.whyItMatters: Distributed systems often cannot maximize both at the same time.
items.whenToApply: Apply when designing shared data paths, multi-region behavior, or critical record updates.
items.guidance: Bias toward consistency for control-heavy or financial flows. Bias toward availability for user-facing systems that can tolerate short-lived staleness.
pros: High availability keeps users working during incidents
cons: Consistency can reduce resilience under partition
items.decisionContext: Distributed data, multi-region traffic, partial failure handling.

items.title: Cost vs performance
items.summary: Balance spend efficiency against responsiveness and throughput.
items.whyItMatters: Performance-heavy architecture can become too expensive, while aggressive savings can make the product feel broken.
items.whenToApply: Apply when the system has compute-heavy APIs, analytics, AI, or latency-sensitive workflows.
items.guidance: Bias toward performance on user-critical paths. Bias toward cost on lower-value or non-urgent workloads.
pros: Higher performance improves user and operator experience
cons: Cost cuts can create slow systems
items.decisionContext: Latency-sensitive traffic, expensive compute, large document or media processing.

items.title: Cost vs reliability
items.summary: Balance lean spend against recovery, redundancy, and outage tolerance.
items.whyItMatters: Reliability controls often cost money, but skipping them can cost more later.
items.whenToApply: Apply when designing backups, failover, recovery, and redundancy.
items.guidance: Bias toward reliability for customer-critical systems and important data. Bias toward cost only when business impact is clearly small.
pros: Lower cost keeps the platform lean
Stronger reliability reduces outage and loss risk
items.decisionContext: Backups, DR planning, redundant services, failover design.

items.title: Speed vs governance
items.summary: Balance fast delivery against consistency, control, and review discipline.
items.whyItMatters: Teams can ship quickly into long-term drift if architecture guardrails stay weak.
items.whenToApply: Apply when platform changes affect many teams or compliance expectations matter.
items.guidance: Bias toward speed for low-risk experiments. Bias toward governance for shared platforms and regulated changes.
cons: Too much speed creates architecture debt
Too much governance slows teams down
items.decisionContext: Platform changes, cross-team design decisions, regulated delivery.

items.title: Security vs usability
items.summary: Balance stronger protection with a workable user and operator experience.
items.whyItMatters: The best control is useless if it blocks legitimate work without cause.
items.whenToApply: Apply when designing authentication, approvals, admin access, or sensitive workflows.
items.guidance: Bias toward security for privileged or sensitive actions. Bias toward usability only after minimum controls are in place.
pros: Better usability lowers friction and support cost
items.decisionContext: Authentication, authorization, admin workflows, upload controls.

items.title: Managed vs self-hosted
items.summary: Choose between operational simplicity and deeper control.
items.whyItMatters: Platform choices shape long-term staffing, risk, and speed.
items.whenToApply: Apply when selecting databases, observability tools, messaging, or AI infrastructure.
items.guidance: Bias toward managed services for MVP and lean teams. Bias toward self-hosted only when control or economics clearly justify it.
items.decisionContext: Platform service selection and operational ownership.

items.title: Build vs buy
items.summary: Choose between owning a custom capability and adopting a packaged one.
items.whyItMatters: Custom development can create long-term burden where a commodity solution would do.
items.whenToApply: Apply when choosing identity, workflow, search, observability, or review tooling.
items.guidance: Bias toward buy when the capability is common and non-differentiating. Bias toward build when it creates strategic advantage.
items.decisionContext: Commodity platform capabilities and differentiating product decisions.

items.title: Simplicity vs scalability
items.summary: Decide when a simple design should grow into a more scalable one.
items.whyItMatters: Overengineering slows teams, but underengineering can break the platform later.
items.whenToApply: Apply when deciding on queues, partitioning, orchestration, or caching.
items.guidance: Bias toward simplicity until clear scale signals appear. Bias toward scalability when load or tenant growth is already visible.
cons: Simple designs may fail under growth
items.decisionContext: Architecture growth, asynchronous patterns, shared bottlenecks.

items.title: Platform leverage vs vendor lock-in
items.summary: Balance speed from native services against future portability.
items.whyItMatters: Cloud-native acceleration is useful, but deep dependence can limit future options.
items.whenToApply: Apply when using proprietary managed services or platform-specific workflows.
items.guidance: Bias toward platform leverage when speed matters most. Bias toward portability when regulation or long-term flexibility is more important.
cons: Too much abstraction can slow teams down
items.decisionContext: Cloud-native services, portability goals, long-term platform strategy.

items.title: Tenant isolation vs operational cost
items.summary: Balance stronger customer separation against simpler shared operations.
items.whyItMatters: SaaS systems must weigh customer risk against platform cost and complexity.
items.whenToApply: Apply when designing multi-tenant data, compute, or document-processing workflows.
items.guidance: Bias toward stronger isolation for enterprise or regulated customers. Bias toward lower-cost sharing only when customer risk is clearly limited.
pros: Strong isolation improves security and compliance
Shared platforms reduce cost and complexity
cons: Sharing can create noisy-neighbor and trust risk
items.decisionContext: Multi-tenant SaaS, shared databases, customer-sensitive workloads.

items.title: ADR template
items.summary: Use a consistent structure for architecture decisions and their consequences.
items.whyItMatters: Clear ADR structure makes trade-offs easier to review and revisit.
items.whenToApply: Apply when findings and recommendations are mature enough to become a decision record.
items.guidance: Capture context, decision, alternatives, trade-offs, consequences, risks, frameworks used, and history.
